Adventure 2. Slicing and Dicing The next evening, instead of being assailed with noise as I approached Tom's garage, I was hit with the delicious smell of hamburgers being grilled. Welcome to McTom's, Tom called out as I came in the door. I hope you're hungry. I noticed the smell was coming from a pan on a hot plate, topped by a lid with wires coming out of it. What do you think? Ask Tom. See, the cover has heating coils in it, so the food cooks on both sides at once. Got tired of waiting for my burgers to cook on one side and then having to flip them over to do the whole process again. Seemed tedious. Makes sense, I nodded. How'd you come up with that one? Simple. Found it right there in box number two. Do the opposite. Food on my hot plate or stove gets heated from the bottom. I just flipped it around so it always gets heated from the top. Hmm. I suppose you're going to tell me that this box has wider implications than just engineering? Absolutely. Trying to do the opposite can take you to some surprisingly creative and effective ideas. Try it. I thought for a moment. Struggling for ideas. Tom, picking up on this, prompted me further. What's something that you, or the book-selling industry in general, have always done in a certain way? Well, we buy books from the publishers, people visit the store, they look at the books, they pick which ones they like, they buy them, and they take the books home with them. Tom's eyes lit up. And what if they didn't buy them? What? What if they didn't buy them? He repeated. What if we turned that around and said people don't buy the books before they take them home? Let's make it work somehow. Don't be afraid to be absurd. It may lead you somewhere brilliant. Okay. People don't buy the books. Instead, we… we… Okay, no, I genuinely don't see how this one could work. Push! Go past your preconceived notions. Well, I racked my brain. I guess we could let customers take the books home without paying to see if they like them or not. But… let's see… we could get their credit card information but not charge it for a week or so. If they don't like the book, they return it and we don't put the charge through. But it would probably never work because people would take advantage of it and… no, Tom interrupted. Tell me how it could work. Oh, it could work, I suppose, if it increased profits by more than whatever we lost by people forgetting or refusing to bring the books back. And the card payment being rejected. Come to think of it, people might be a whole lot more willing to take a book home with them knowing they wouldn't have to pay for it if they didn't like it. And people rarely don't like a book once they've started reading it. As we say in the lab, said Tom, maybe it's worth an experiment. You don't have to commit to it forever. Try it for a day or two. That seems reasonable. What other ways can we look at it? I asked, my skin prickling with excitement at all the ideas I was coming up with. Well, there are many things you can do to an object or process besides reversing it. Say, I think the burgers are ready. Tom took the two hamburgers out of the pan and put them on buns. Then he pulled out an old-fashioned vegematic from beneath the work table. Onions, he asked as he began slicing and dicing an onion, do the opposite as one of a series of four boxes from what I call my vegematic or slice and dice boxes. Great invention by the way, the vegematic. So simple yet so useful. Anyway, the next slice and dice box is number three. Divide something up. Tom began rummaging through one of the cabinets and pulled out a box about the size of a desk drawer. Do you ski? He asked. A little. Why? Suddenly the thing I always really hated about skiing was lugging skis around, having to attach them to the top of the car and then carry them around the lodge and to the slopes. So I set out to solve the problem. I got to the divide something up box and bingo. Tom then pulled out two accordion plexiglass strips from the box, which he proceeded to unfold into a pair of full-sized skis. As each section unfolded, they locked into place with a reassuring click. There you go. He said, handing them to me. I wouldn't use them to compete at the Alpine World Cup, but they're sturdy enough for my leisurely meanderings down the mountain. Tom pulled out a small bag from the crisper of his mini-fridge. Carrots, he asked. Sure, I love baby carrots. Actually, these are not baby carrots, clarified Tom. Most people think these little carrots are specially grown miniature carrots. But what they are is a great example of dividing it up. The story is that a few years ago, the owner of a carrot processing plant in California had a problem. He had to discard tons of misshapen carrots every day. So he came up with the idea of dividing these unwanted carrots into small 2-inch pieces, rolling them around to smooth them up nicely and voila! Baby carrots were born and caught on like wildfire. Now, he continued excitedly, how might we slice and dice your business for fun and profit? Inside this box, you can either divide something into separate parts or sections, or, if it's already in sections, you can divide it up even more. That might have some implications for my business. Of course, a bookstore is divided into sections, so I could increase the division by upping the number of sections, which might make it easier for people to find books on their specific interests. Good, said Tom. Now, what's not sectional that you could improve by dividing it more? Let's see. How about this? We've been selling quite a few books online lately. I bet that part of our business would really take off if more energy were focused on it. Maybe I could make that into a separate section or profit center and let Robert devote himself full time to it. Same with our institutional business, where we sell books to companies in bulk. I could block that off into a separate profit center and put Nancy in charge of it. I bet it could really make a difference if we had devoted attention to these sectors. Does that count as dividing it up? It sure does, but don't worry about that. As long as you're coming up with ideas, that's all that matters. Yeah, I guess you're right. This is fun. Let's keep going. Okay, Tom replied with a grin. Give me another problem to solve that would help get your bookstore back on its feet. Shoplifters, I blurted out. I lose a small fortune to them every year. Good. Let's look at how we might solve that problem with box number four. Take something out. What can you remove from an object or process that's keeping you from getting the results you want? Or how can you extract only that part or characteristic you really want from an object or process? Take the shoplifters out and shoot them? I replied. Only half joking. Excellent. Laugh, Tom. Humor is an essential part of the creative process. Loosens up the mind. This unexpected connections makes the process more enjoyable. Even such a seemingly off-the-cuff remark could lead to some good ideas. Like what? I asked eagerly. Well, it could lead to ideas on how you might prosecute shoplifters more vigorously. Or perhaps you really should put up a sign that reads, shoplifters will be shot. If nothing else, it's sure to grab attention. Gosh, you don't waste an idea, do you? Nope. Nothing, absolutely anything, can lead you to a good idea. How else might you take out the shoplifters? And remember, sometimes things are taken out by never being allowed in the first place. Hey, here's an idea. What if my bookstore could be by invitation only? So you have to be invited to get in. That would keep out the shoplifters and lend a certain exclusivity that bigger bookstores can never offer. Now you're talking. Tom said. If you could try it on a day or evening, you're not normally open. And see how it works. Start with an invitation-only event. And if it works well, you can consider moving toward an invitation-only or membership-based model. Kind of like building a prototype, right? Exactly. Now you're starting to think like an engineer. Amazing. What's the next box? I asked, not wanting to waste a moment with my new mentor. Here's another one for you. We've divided things up and taken things out. Now let's combine things in box number five. Bring some things together. In fact, this box spurred one of my favorite stories about someone using my idea system. Oh, tell me more. I insisted. A friend of mine runs a nursing home. I showed him how to think inside the box like I'm showing you. He had a problem with two of his more senile residents. One woman kept grabbing food off other people's plates and was driving her fellow diners crazy. The other, a man, thought people were poisoning his food, and his accusations were likewise causing quite a commotion. My friend looked in the various boxes. He was almost ready to use the first box and separate these two troublemakers from the rest of the residents during meals. But then, he looked in this box. Bring some things together. And what do you think he did? Put them together? I guess the only logical answer. Yes, and he did it in an absolutely brilliant way. Before seeding them together at meals, he told the man who thought his food was being poisoned that the woman had volunteered to be his food taster and to make sure his food was safe. He was happy to have her test his food periodically. And now that the woman could take food from someone else without causing an uproar, she stopped bothering the other residents. We inventors are always putting this together with that. Taking a doodad from here and matching it with a doohickey from there. Ben Franklin combined reading glasses with long distance glasses and came up with bifocals. Apple combined downloading music onto a computer hard drive with portability and headphones and came up with the iPod. It's a great way to come up with new products or services or new ways to market yourself. Yet, businesses tend to operate in a very isolated, non-combination way. For example, why can't you and the shop next door combine your efforts and do a joint promotion? What do they sell? It's a jewelry store, so it wouldn't work because remember, interrupted Tom, no pessimism. Only what is possible. What if you ran an ad that read, beautify her body and her mind. Show her you love her for both. This week only. Buy a piece of jewelry and get your choice of any book. That's good. I said, scribbling it down on a scrap of paper. Don't worry about writing everything down. Tom reassured me. What's important is the boxes and getting into the habit of using them. If you do, believe me, you'll soon be generating ideas faster than you can use them. I am already, I said. I could team up with all sorts of businesses by advertising with them and maybe even sending out promotions to their mailing lists. Putting things together makes me think about how I could take advantage of current events by displaying books according to topics currently in the news. I could also, Tom interrupted again. I think you've got this box down Pat. Why don't you stop by again tomorrow and I'll tell you what you can learn from the Matryoshka. Is that a box? Yes. And a doll. Huh? You'll see.
